meaning: 'toward, near to, near, to, by, at, in, with (place), in the presence of, in reference to, in comparison with'; la-haba 'away to, unto, toward, at'; ba-haba 'among, at, upon, by, in, wheresoever, near, with (at), to, toward, from, in the presence of, before, in comparison with'; bo-haba 'somewhere, there is'; bo-haba ... bo-haba 'at one moment ... at another'; ǝmhaha 'from (with), from the presence of, from before, from where, by means of'; ǝnta haba 'through'; ǝska haba 'as far as'; haba haba 'more and more'; as relative, adverb: haba 'where, wheresoever, whither'; za-haba 'where, in which, the place where (also without makān or mǝdər)'; haba...ḥayya (zǝya) 'wherein'; ba-haba 'where, there where, in which'; ǝnta haba 'wheresoever, the same way, by which'; ǝm haba, ǝmǝnna haba 'whence, from where'; ǝska haba 'to where, whither'; the suffix pronouns are attached to the base habe-; 'until'. For the initial ha, cp. also Soq. ḥa 'here', ḥak 'there'.
origin_explanation: Dillmann 1907:394 analyzes haba as coming from ha-ba 'in-there'; so also Rundgren 1955:47 who identifies ḥa with ḥa of kaḥa 'yonder, away' even though the correspondence ḥ:ḥ is not normal; however, the meaning of ḥa is not clearly indicated. The connection between ḥaba and SAr. b-br suggested by Praetorius, ZDMG 57 (1903), 272 is unlikely regardless of the possible identity of meaning. Praetorius 1879:266 relies on Ludolf's spelling of the Amharic preposition h < k as ḥ and connects it with G. ḥa of ḥaba. Cp. rather kab 'from', and for the final b, see also Tna. nab 'toward', kǝṣab.

meaning: 'hide, conceal'; ʾaḥbǝʾa (K*), causative; taḥabǝʾa, passive, reflexive; ḥabāʾi, act. part. of ḥabǝʾa; ḥǝbuʾ 'hidden, concealed, secret'; (za-ḥǝbuʾ 'mystery'); (ba-ḥabuʾ, ḥǝbwa 'secretly'); (tǝmḥǝrta ḥǝbuʾāt 'Doctrine of Mysteries, the Mystagogia'; see Lifchitz 19-85; Hammer-Schmidt-Six 1983:55; Strelcyn 1974:65); ḥǝbuʾāwi 'concealed, clandestine'; ḥǝbʾat 'hiding' (n.); mǝḥbāʾ 'hiding place, hidden place, secret place, receptacle'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ḥabāʾa 'hide', SAr. ḥb, Heb. ḥb (ḥifʿil), Aram. ḥabā (ḥb), Syr. ḥubyā 'darkness' (Brockelmann 1928:209), Md. ḥba 'hide', Akk. ḥa-bu. ETHIOPIC: Tna. ḥabǝʾ 'hide', Te. ḥabǝʾa, ḥambǝʾa, Amh. abba 'hide', ḥǝbuʾ 'concealed, hidden' (from Geez).
SEMITIC: Ar. ḥabba 'be deceitful'. See also hababa II.

meaning: (K*T), hababa (M.T. in the reprint) 'press (grapes)'; māḥ(ǝ)bab, māḥ(ǝ)baḥt, māḥ(ǝ)babt (pl. ma-ḥābab), (M.T) māḥ(ǝ)bab, māḥ(ǝ)babt 'clay vessel, oil cellar, winepress'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: The comparison with Akk. ḥabābu 'murmur, chirp', Ar. ḥabba 'rage, dash' suggested by von Soden 301, with reservations, is unlikely. Also unlikely is the connection with Heb. gǝb 'pit, ditch', Ar. ğubb suggested by Schulthess 55, n. 17.
ETHiOPiC: Tna. hanbaba '(with augmented n) 'make fall'.
habbab, (T) habhab 'one who swears'.

meaning: (T,K) see tahabala; tahabala, (T) tahabala 'be bold, dare, be daring, be arrogant, be impudent, be presumptuous'; ahabala (K*) 'dare someone, challenge'; tahabala (K), reciprocal of tahabala; astahabala (K*) 'dare someone, challenge'; astahabala (K*), causative of tahabala; habali 'bold, daring'; habul, (KG) habbul 'bold, daring, arrogant, presumptuous'; habl (K*) 'boldness, arrogance'; mahabl (K*) 'who challenges, who dares someone'; mastahabl 'who challenges'; tahabali 'arrogant, audacious'; tahablo(t) 'boldness, bold behavior, audacity, arrogance'.
origin_explanation: according to another source the b is geminated throughout. SEMiTiC: perhaps Ar. habila 'be of unsound mind'. Heb. habal-im 'arrogant ones' (as in Ps. 140:6; see Dahood, Biblica 45[1964].407). ETHiOPiC: Tna. habara 'be presumptuous' (with alternance of liquids, and h for h).

meaning: (T,M) habbane, (Lt) habbane 'hood, headcloth worn by priests, turban, hem, fringe, border of a garment, collar' (see Hammerschmidt 1970:155)
origin_explanation: SEMiTiC: for 'fringes, borders of a garment', cp. Ar. habana 'hem, tuck, and sew a garment for shortening it'. ETHiOPiC: Tna. habbane 'turban worn by priests', Amh. habbane, abbane (from Geez).

meaning: (yahbar) 'be connected (with), be associated (with), associate (with), coincide, join, participate, ally one-self, collaborate, consent, agree (with), be in common, conspire (against)'. With another verb (in the gerund) it means 'do jointly, do together'; tahabra 'be associated (with), be connected (with)'; tahabra 'join one another'; tahabara (Gr), caus. of tahabara; also 'harmonize'; habari (K*), act. part. of habara; habur 'associated, joined, conjoined, united, connected'; (habura halläwe 'of like nature or substance'); habura (adv.) 'together, jointly, all together, at the same time, in unison'; hubare, (T) hubare 'union, unity, conjunction, association'; habr 'crowd, (Lt) unit of soldiers belonging to a territory'; habrat 'unison, union, joining, community, association, agreement, consensus, accord, harmony, collection'; (habrata zemä 'harmony [in song])'; (ba-habrat 'unanimously'); 'ahbaro 'agreement'; (mashafa 'ahbaro 'a homily for the feast of St. George and Mary'); (ba-'ahbaro 'unanimously'); (ba-'ahbaro qäl 'with a unanimous voice'); mäh(a)bar 'association, society, company, choir, community, assembly, multitude, crowd, congregation, gathering, council, convent, monastery'; (beta mäh(a)bar 'monastery, convent'); mäh(a)bart 'company, (Lt) joint, articulation'; mäh(a)barawi 'who lives in a convent, monk'; tahabari 'agreeing, harmonious, united'; tahabrot, in batahabrot 'in unison, together'; *mhbr, see below.
origin_explanation: with, be in the company of'. Md. hbr, Ug. hbr 'companion'. Ph. hbr, Akk. ebru, ibru 'companion, friend'. See on this root Wagner, in Baumgartner 1967, p.360-1.F. Rosenthal (A history of Muslim historiography, 1951, p. 10, n. 2) makes a distinction between h/hbr (Heb. Ethiopic) 'join, connect' and hbr (Heb) 'associate, colleague'. There may be different roots hbr and hbr, but it seems to me that the meanings 'join, connect, associate, colleague' may go back to a single root. F. Rosenthal derives Ar. habar 'information' from hbr 'connect'. Note that Ar. habara 'inform, give news' is connected by Finkelstein, JBL 75 (1956). 328ff with habaru 'be noisy, make noise'. ETHiOPiC: Tna. habarä 'be joined (to a group), in agreement'. Te. habra 'assemble'. Amh.Gur. abbara. For G. mäh(a)bar, cp. Tna. mahbar, Te. mahbar, Amh. mahbbar, mabär, Gur. mabär; passed into Cushitic: Bil. habar 'unite'.

meaning: (Lt) 'portion (of land, ground, city, camp), side (of mountain, valley), cord'
origin_explanation: SEMiTiC: Ar. hubra 'portion, share'.

habrat 'enchantment, magic spell'. SEMiTiC: Heb. habar 'conjure', Aram-Syr. habbara 'conjuror, charmer', Aram. habber 'charm', Ph. hbr 'magic'. The various Hebrew dictionaries connect Heb. hbr 'magic' with the root hbr 'tie, bind', a notion of tying referring to the magical practice of tying knots. Finkelstein, JBL 75 (1956).328ff., connects Heb. hbr 'magic, charm' with Akk. habaru 'be noisy', huburu 'uproar', the charmer being the one who mutters sounds. ETHiOPiC: Har. habär 'curse'. Gur. abär-än säno (lit. 'make a curse') is borrowed from Cushitic: Som. habär 'curse', Sa.Af. 'abär.
habbasi (M) 'baker'; mähbäs (Lt) 'bakery'; mähabbäs 'bakery, bakehouse'. denominative from habast 'bread', for which habaza.

meaning: (T. In the reprint) mäh(a)bat 'for closing, means of connecting or joining together'
origin_explanation: ETHiOPiC: Amh. abbätä 'tie into a bundle'.

meaning: (Lt) 'kind of vase' (the quoted passage in Dillmann 602 has habay);
origin_explanation: from Aram-Syr. habbaya 'wine jug' > Ar. habiya 'barrel' (see, however, Kaufman 53) > G. habay (Fraenkel 168). Zimmern 33 thinks that the origin of the above-mentioned nouns is Akk. habu 'jug'.

yahbaza (K*), causative; tahabza, passive; habazi (pl. habaziyan, habazt) 'baker'; habuz 'baked'; habz (T.M), habz (K*) 'bread'; habast, (pl. habawaz), (T) habast (coming from habazt, with z > s by assimilation) 'bread, piece of bread, loaf of bread, flat wheaten bread with thirteen crosses imprinted on the surfaces; when given at communion, it is broken so that each communicant receives a piece with a cross on it)'; (beta habast [Lt] 'bakery'); mäh(a)baz 'oven, bakery'; mähabbäz 'bakery, bakehouse'. SEMiTiC: Ar. hubz 'bread' (considered by Noeldeke 1910:56 a Geez loanword as against Landberg 1920:553ff). Mh. haböz 'bake', habz 'bread'. The connection with Heb. habittim 'pans for baking' (from a non-existing hbt) suggested by Buhl 213 on the authority of Gesenius is unlikely. ETHiOPiC: Te. habbäzät 'thick round bread'. Tna. habasti, Amh. abbäzä 'bake bread', abbäzt 'a small loaf of bread', habast, abast (from Geez); passed into Cushitic: Qem. abbäza 'woman who bakes bread', Kham. ebez 'make cook'. The derivation of Amh. amäzza (also amizza) 'bread made of a mixture of flours that are beginning to turn sour' from G. habaza 'bake', suggested by Praetorius 1879:58, is unlikely. For a possible Cushitic origin of amäzza, see Brockelmann 1950:21. See also kobaza, and *hmz.

meaning: (L) hadafa 'row, steer (a boat), be a pilot, direct, lead, guide, teach, devise, (T,Y,M) save, cure, polish, render clean'
origin_explanation: (zayəhaddaf, zayəheddaf) 'pilot';

meaning: 'oar, rudder', (T) haddāf 'oar, rudder, governance, direction, (T) rower, teacher'; (za-haddāf) 'rower, pilot'; haddāfi 'rower, steersman, pilot'; hadduf (K*) 'pass. part. of hadafa; haddāfa 'governance'; mahaddāf 'oar, rudder, (KG) means of polish-ing'; mahaddāf (K*) 'rower, steersman'; mahaddāf 'place of steering'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. gadafa 'row', gādif 'rower'. For G. gadafa, hadafa and Ar. gadafa, qadafa, qadafa, qadafa, see Fraenkel 227-8, and Noeldeke 1910:61-2. For a possible relation between q:h, cp. qafara III, and hasara. ETHIOPIC: Tna. qāzāfā (with alternace d:z), Te. qāzfa, qāzfa, Amh. qāzzāfā. While 'polish, render clean' of G. hadafa could have developed from a meaning such as 'save, cure', that is, 'make neat, clean', it is also possible that these meanings belong to a root hadafa II.

meaning: 'release, remission (of sins or debt), immunity, forgiveness, pardon'; haddāgāt, had(d)āgāt 'divorce, state of divorce, dismissal, exemption'; (mashafa haddāgāt 'bill of divorce'); hadigot 'abandonment'; hadgān, same; tahadgo (T) 'omission, release';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hadaga 'miscarry, cast her young before time (she-camel)', Sar. hdg 'leave'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. hadaga 'abandon', Te. hadga 'addaga 'let go' (from Geez), Gaf. adaga 'ab-throw away'. Guidl 688 and Baeteman 934 Amh. dug 'representative of a chief during sence' from hdg, dug, for *adug, passive par-cp. also Te. hadug 'inspector', Amh. hadug 'patriarch's deputy' (from Geez). The identi-of hadaga with Amh. kādda 'deny' (< *ho hada'a < hadaga) suggested by Rundgren 1955:74 is unlikely. The root also occurs in Cushitic. hadag 'chase away', Bil. adag 'take away'.
denominative from haddāg 'top and bottom margins'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. addāgā 'straighten by cutting'.

meaning: 'reside, dwell, lodge, abide, inhabit, halt, stay overnight'; (hadara lā-rə'əla 'reside in, possess (of demon spirit in a person)'); 'ahadara, causative; also 'lodge (tr.), settle'; tahadara 'be inhabited'; tahadara 'live together, make a treaty with one another'; 'astahadara 'cause to dwell, place (a corner-stone), cause to be in a certain condition, prepare, (Lt) give a chronic disease'; hadāri 'sojourner, lodger, family'; hadur 'set, fixed, who dwells, who stays over-night, residing, living, who inhabits, that is placed'; hidr (Lt) 'inside of an apartment of women'; hadrat 'dwelling, residing, sojourn, sojourn-ing'; hadāriy (Lt) 'hospitable'; hadāri, act. part. of 'ahdara; ('ahdāre 'əngədā 'hospitable'); māh(ə)dār (pl. mahādər, māh(ə)dārāt) 'place of habitation, dwelling, habitation, lodging, abode, resting place, residence, room, taber-nacle, tent, leather case for keeping a book, box, lunar station'; mastahadār (Lt) 'that protects property, (T.M) lodging-house, who lodges, innkeeper, (Lt) that gives a chronic disease'; mastahadāri (Lt) 'that gives a chronic disease'; mastahadāri (Lt) 'black magic, formula of a charm, specific prayer of secret names (ma-tāt) to protect a lost animal from being at-tacked by wild animals'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hadara 'remain and keep to a place', hidr 'women's quarters of a tent', Sar. hdr 'cham-ber', m-hdr 'residence', Heb. heder 'room', Ph. hdr '(burial) chamber', Ug. hdr 'room' (with h). ETHIOPIC: Tna. hadara 'spend the night', Te. hadara 'dwell, stay', Har. hadara, Amh.Gur.Gaf. addara, Arg. addara. For mastahadārt, cp. Tna. mastahadār. The root passed into Cushitic: Bil. hadar 'settle', Sa.Af. hadar 'spend the night'.

meaning: 'one who washes'; hadab 'washed'; hadb (Lt) 'washing'; hadb (Lt) 'soap'; hadbat 'washing (n.)'; haddāb 'washing (n.), water with which one washes, wash water'; hadabā 'a washing, a bathing'; hadbat 'washing (n.), ablution'; māh(ə)dāb 'bath, basin'; māh(ə)dāb 'place where one washes, bath, bathing place, basin'; māhdabi 'one who washes'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Dillmann 624 compares the Geez root with Ar. hadaba 'dye, color'. ETHIOPIC: Te. hasba 'wash', Tna. hasābā, Har. ha-taba, Arg. hattāba, Amh. attābā, Gur. atābā, Gaf. asābā.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. hadar, Amh. hadar, Gur. ədar pas-sed into Cushitic: Qab. Tem. hidāra. Had. idāra. In view of G. hadar, the connection with Heb. 'adar suggested by D. Cohen 1970:10 is unlikely.

meaning: 'a small amount, little, a little while, few in number, a small quantity, small matter, trifle, a thing of no value, small'; hadātā, ba-hadāt 'a little, a little bit, a little while, in small quantities, in few words'; hadāt (Lt) 'small in number'; hadātāt 'few, few in number'.
origin_explanation: Dillmann 622 derives it from hatāt, from htt; so also Brockelmann 1908:237 (see hattat). The derivation from whd 'become few, be little' suggested by Noel-deke 1904:30 would be possible were it not for the final t. The final t is perhaps to be explained by assimilation to the initial h.

meaning: (T) hasaba, (KG) hadaba 'wash, wash away'; 'ahadaba, causative; tahadba 'bathe, wash oneself, wash the body, be washed'; 'astahadba 'ask to prepare a bath'.

'be shy, shun, be ashamed, be embarrassed, be confounded, be disgraced, fear, revere, reverence'; 'ahfara 'put to shame'; tahafra, tahafara 'be put to shame, be covered with shame, blush with shame, be ashamed, be confounded, be disgraced, be offended, be humiliated'; 'astahafara 'shame, put to shame, disgrace, dishonor, confound, humiliate, offend, vilify'; haffār, haffāri 'modest, shy, ashamed, shame-ful'; (ihafāri 'shameless, impudent'); hafur 'ashamed, shamefaced, disgraced, (Gr) infamous, dishonored'; hufāre (K.Gr.210) 'obscenity, impurity, infamy, dishonor'; hafrat 'shame, blush, turpitude, ignominy, impropriety, disgrace, dishonor'; (hafrata 'əgā' 'genitals'); mahfāri, (K*) 'əhfāri 'what causes shame, what puts to shame'; mahfār 'genitals'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hafira 'be timid, be shy, be bashful'. Heb. hāper, Aram hāpar, Syr. hāpar 'be ashamed'. O. Kapeliuk suggests Heb. herpa 'shame' (with metathesis).

ETHIOPIC: Tna. hafara 'be ashamed, be shy', Te. hafara 'be in awe', Amh. affara 'be shy, be timid', Arg. affara, Gur. in affar 'bashful', passed into Cushitic. Kham. hafar. Reinisch 1887:186 also compares Bil. hobar (with alternance b:f) 'become sad owing to shame', habar-is 'put to shame'.

meaning: (Lt) 'protect, defend'; mah(a)far (pl. mahafar) 'friend, client, acquaintance, relative'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hafara 'protect', SAR. hfr 'protection', Sh. h-hefer 'ask someone to give protection', Heb. hpr (pu'al) 'be protected'. Brockelmann, ZS 8 (1932). 112 gives it a basic meaning 'to cover'; see hfr III.
hfr III could be connected with hfr II with the basic meaning 'to cover' > headcover, protection'. It is also related to mafart, for which see afara II.

meaning: (D.Y.M) 'scratch, engrave, design, imprint, strike, write'; hafatat, hafatat (T.Ts.Lt) 'engraving, imprint'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to hbt, hbt (so also Dillmann 628): Ar. habata 'beat, strike', Sar. hbt, Heb. habat, Aram. habat, Syr. habat, Md. hbt 'batter down, beat down, strike upon'. See also hanfata I, and fahata.
to be identified with hag'la, above.
From Ar. hawf(a) 'peach, plum'; also Tna. kaf 'peach', Te.Amh. kok. See also kok.

meaning: (D 606) 'door, doorway, gate, portal, leaf of door'; (madraka hohat 'threshold')
origin_explanation: for hawhaw 'gates' used in astronomy, see O. Neugebauer, Orientalia 33 (1964).49-71. SEMITIC: Ar. hawha 'aperture in a wall, small doorway between houses' (considered by Noeldeke 1910:51 a Geez loanword, but in view of Sar. hh. below, this may be not the case), Sar. hh 'passageway, corridor' (?), Heb. hawh-im 'hollows, recesses', Mh. ho 'mouth, opening'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. hohat 'blank space between letters' (from Geez).

meaning: (K*), hallada (T) 'last long, gather, accumulate'; tahallada (D.Lt) 'adhere to, be applied to'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. halada 'last long, remain forever'.

meaning: (K), hallada (T.Y.M) 'accomplish, exterminate, appoint to the office of the executioner, (Gr.4) order'; tahallada (K), tahallada (T.M), tahallada (Gr.4), tahallada (L) 'be refuted'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Har. heläda 'finish, accomplish an end'. Tna. haläda 'lose one's position (that is be terminated)'.
transcription of Heb. holed.

meaning: (T) halafa 'pass, pass by, pass away (from), pass through, pass over, cross over, depart, step aside from, go beyond, transgress, perish, die'; 'ahlafa, causative; also 'pass (time)'; tahalafa 'pass on, go by'; tahalafa 'wander to and fro'; 'astahalafa 'make pass, put off, leave out'; 'astahalafa 'make (the hands) pass over each other, send around, cross, transfer, adjourn'; halafi, act. part. of halafa; also 'passerby, transitory, transient, temporal, temporary'; haluf 'who passes, who crosses, who trespasses'; (haluf gasso la- 'who aims toward'); halufa 'while passing'; halafiyat 'people traveling or passing by, crowd'; halafiyat, halafiyat (pl. of halafit) 'things that pass away, transitory things'; halafat 'passing, passing by, passing away, passage, departure, transitoriness, death'; mah(a)laf, mah(a)laft 'passing, pass, pass-word'
origin_explanation: mahlaf 'passage, place for crossing or passing through'; SEMITIC: Ar. halafa 'follow, succeed', hallafa 'leave behind', SAR. hlf 'passage between hills', Sh. ho-t-lef 'change', Heb. halaf 'pass', Aram. halap 'pass by' (pa'el) 'change', Syr halap 'exchange, substitute' (pa'el) 'change, exchange', Md. hlp. ETHIOPIC: Tna. haläfa 'pass', Te. halfa, Har. huluf, bäya, Arg. halläfa, Amh.Gaf.Gur. alläfa; passed into Cushitic: Sa. halaf 'pass'.

SEMITIC: Ar. halla 'be lean, be destitute', Heb. halal 'be troubled, be pained, tremble' (Kaddary, VT 13[1963].486-9). ETHIOPIC: Te. halläla 'render unable', tahalläla 'be unable, be tired', Amh. aläll zäläll alä 'be unstable, be unsettled'; passed into Cushitic: Bil. halal-s 'become tired'.

meaning: (T) halqa 'be consumed, be wasted, perish, cease, disappear, come to an end, be accomplished, be terminated, be finished, be destroyed, fail, dwindle away, be spent, die, be decided upon, be determined'; 'ahlqa, causative; also 'bring to an end, cease (tr.), accomplish, finish, destroy, exterminate, spend, lay waste, use up, annihilate, decree, decide'; tahalqa, passive of 'ahlqa; tahalqa, reciprocal of 'ahlqa; 'astahalqa, causative of tahalqa; also 'come to a settlement'; haluq 'finished, accomplished, destroyed, decided upon, precise'; (matura wahaluqa 'resolutely'); halq 'destruction, end'; halqat 'end, consummation, dwindling, completion, performance, execution, death'; mah(a)lqt 'ceasing, end, conclusion, ruin, loss, death'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. haliqa 'be shabby (clothes)', halqa 'wear out (a garment)', Heb. hilleq 'destroy', Ug. hlq 'perish', Akk. halaqu 'disappear, perish'. Praetorius, ZDMG 61 (1907). 624 compares Ar. galiqa 'disappear', Soq. 'eylaq. ETHIOPIC: Tna. haläqa 'finish, be finished', Amh. alläqa 'come to an end, be consumed'.

meaning: 'count, number, enumerate, take account, review, impute, consider'; 'ah"allqa (K*), causative; tah"allqa, passive; also 'be regarded as'; (zaviyyoth elq "countless, innumerable"); h"allqi (K*), act. part. of h"allqa; h"alluq, h"alluq "numbered, counted, reckoned'; h"alqa, h"alqa (pl. h"alqa) "number, calculation, enumeration, sum"; (vorit zah"alqa, "orita h"alqa "the Book of Numbers"); (valbo h"alqa, za'anbala h"alqa "innumerable, boundless, limitless"); (ba-h"alqa "according to the number"); h"allqe "enumeration, quantity, numbering, counting, number"
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. halqa 'measure, proportion', Heb. halaq 'divide', Aram. halaq 'allot', Syr. halaq 'allot', halqa 'lot, portion', Md. hlq 'allot, assign'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. halq 'number, count', Amh. alq, alq 'number', Har. heläqa 'count, consider', Gur. eläqa.

meaning: (KG) hallat 'cane on which the cross is nailed and carried by the deacon, reed, stubble, chaff, papyrus, (Lt) pole, long spear'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: the root is undoubtedly hll. If there is an element of the cane or stalk being hollow, cp. Heb. halal 'cavity', Aram. halila, Syr. halula. The connection with Syr. helta (or hellata) 'sheath', Ar. hilla(t) suggested by Brockelmann 1928: 232 is unlikely. It is tempting to connect it with Heb. halil 'flute', but in view of Har. ullulu 'flute' and also of Qabenna (Cushitic) ullilli-ta one wonders whether the root is not onomatopoeic and is common to Semitic and Cushitic. ETHIOPIC: Tna. hallät 'cane on which the cross is nailed', Amh. hallät 'staff, rod' (from Geez), Te. häl 'kind of cane'. Tna. hall 'passed into Cushitic: Sa. halat 'reed, rush'.

meaning: 'watch, keep watch, guard', (H 218 has hallawa = Amh. + 'be deep'; it is a misprint for + 'guard') hallawi 'custodian'; *hallaw. (Gr.203) hallaw 'watchman, night guard, custodian';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hlw 'save, deliver', related to Ar. hla (hwl) 'take care of'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. hallawa 'watch', Amh. allawa, allawa 'night watch'.

meaning: 'consider, think, ponder, keep in mind, meditate, look after someone, take care of, watch, reason, reflect upon, turn over in one's mind, perceive, decide, devise, imagine'; 'ahallaya, ahallaya, causative; also 'remind, suggest'; tahallaya, passive; also 'be conceived, be mindful of'; 'astahallaya 'cause to keep under control'; hallayi, act. part. of hallaya; hallay (M), pass. part. of hallaya; also 'meditated, thought, considered'; hallaya 'faculty of thinking, intellect'; hallayo (t) 'opinion, intellect, mind, meditation'; hallina 'thinking, faculty of thinking, mind, thought, understanding, intelligence, intellect, reasoning, reason, device, imagination, intention, conscience, proposal, opinion'; (muqata hallina 'enthusiasm'); hallinat 'conscience'; hallinawi 'pertaining to thought';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. (ta)hayyala 'imagine', Heb. hla (hly) 'think, care for' (Driver, JTS 29[1928].392; Eitan, HUCA 12-13[1937-8].82-3), Talmudic Aram. in la holi wala margis (Eitan, ib). ETHIOPIC: Tna. hallaya 'think', Amh. hallayo 'theory' (from Geez); Tna. hallina 'think' (denominative from hallina 'thought'). Gur. atyana (at-stem of ana) 'consider' (for n:l, see Leslau 1979:8.23). Cohen 1939:34 following Praetorius 1879:7 identified Amh. ayya 'see' with G. hly 'think', but see Praetorius 1879:510 and Leslau 1979:123 who connect Amh. ayya 'see' with the Semitic root hzy 'see'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. ambo, ambo 'brackish water from a mineral spring'; also in Cushitic: Bil. hambo 'cistern'.
from Ar. hamr 'wine', but hamrawi could also be another spelling for hamrawi and be connected with hamr 1, above.

phonetics: hammassa 'make five' (denominative) tahammassa (K*) 'become five'; hams, hams 'five' (with a feminine noun) hams-a 'five times'; hammus 'quintuple, fivefold'; hammastu 'five' (with a masculine and feminine noun); the suffix pronouns are attached to hammasti-; hmas (fem. hmast) 'fifth'; hm (a)sawi, hm(a)say 'fifth'; (hm(a)say 'the fifth time'); hm(a)sit 'fifth part of the kikros' (for see below); hamus 'five (days), the fifth day (of the week or month), Thursday'; (hamus mawala 'five days'); ('ama hamusu lasarq 'the fifth day of the month'); hamsa 'fifty'; (baila hamsa 'Pentecost'); hummase, hammase 'the five, reunion of five, the becoming five'; mahammas (KG) 'that has five sides'; mahammas 'five times'; tahmas, tahmas 'quintupling, number five of five'; tahmast 'fivefold, five times, five parts';
meaning: 'make five' (denominative) tahammassa (K*) 'become five'; hams, hams 'five' (with a feminine noun) hams-a 'five times'; hammus 'quintuple, fivefold'; hammastu 'five' (with a masculine and feminine noun); the suffix pronouns are attached to hammasti-; hmas (fem. hmast) 'fifth'; hm (a)sawi, hm(a)say 'fifth'; (hm(a)say 'the fifth time'); hm(a)sit 'fifth part of the kikros' (for see below); hamus 'five (days), the fifth day (of the week or month), Thursday'; (hamus mawala 'five days'); ('ama hamusu lasarq 'the fifth day of the month'); hamsa 'fifty'; (baila hamsa 'Pentecost'); hummase, hammase 'the five, reunion of five, the becoming five'; mahammas (KG) 'that has five sides'; mahammas 'five times'; tahmas, tahmas 'quintupling, number five of five'; tahmast 'fivefold, five times, five parts';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hams 'five', SAr. hms, Soq. hamos, Heb. hmes, Aram. hamsa, Syr. ham(m)es, Md. hamsa, Ph. hms, Akk. hamsu. ETHIOPIC: Tna. hammuste 'five', hamas 'the fifth order of the vowels', Te. hams 'five', Har. hammisti, Ammasta, Amh.Arg. ammast, Gur. amast.

phonetics: hamaya (yahmi), hammaya (T.Y) hamaya 'chain, tie, bind, shackle'; 'ahamaya, causative; tahamaya, passive;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. hama (hmy) 'deny, interdict' that is 'restrain > tie, bind', Akk. ham 'immobilize, paralyze'.
for mahabbaz (with alternation of labials), from 'bake'. Reinsch 1887:194, under hamad, records G. hamaza 'bake', not attested elsewhere.

phonetics: hanbaz (T) hanbaz 'instrument of torture, (T) forked pole';
origin_explanation: Dillmann 602 records it under hbz II. K gives habbaz and hanbaz; however, in all the cited examples the form is hanbaz. ETHIOPIC: Littmann-Hofner 83 compare it, with reservations, with Te. hanobaza 'make noise', hanobaz 'confusion'.

from Ar. hanafiyy, of Syriac (hanpa) origin (Noeldeke 1910:35). On Ar. hanif, see Noeldeke 1910:30, Jeffery 112-5. The root exists in a related meaning in Heb. hanep 'be polluted, be alienated from God'.

phonetics: hanfata 'scratch, scrape, engrave, carve'; 'ahanfata (Lt), causative; tahanfata, passive;
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. hanfata 'scratch', Te. hanfaa, ha-fasa, undoubtedly also Gur. affata (for t:t, see Leslau 1979:15.2). The root is augmented by n in relation to hafata. See also hanfata I.

phonetics: hangara (D), hangara (Y.M) 'carry on the shoulders'; hangari (Lt), act. participle;
origin_explanation: Dillmann 605 connects Amh. akokko ala (translation of hangara) with akokko 'rock hyrax' and seems to give the meaning of 'rock hyrax' to hangara. The Amharic expression akokko ala may be connected with the name of the animal akokko, but the expression akokko ala means 'carry on the back'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. hangara 'carry on the back', Te. hangara.
seems to be a transcription of Heb. higger, with an inserted n.

phonetics: hanaqa (yhnaq) 'strangle, choke, throttle, take by the throat, put on the neck'; 'ahanaqa (K*), causative; tahanaqa, passive, reflexive; tahanaqa (DTW 944), reciprocal; hanaq 'strangled, choked'; hanqat, verbal noun of hanaqa; mahanaq (K*) 'that strangles'; mahanaq (Lt) 'means of strangling or choking';
meaning: 'strangle, choke, throttle, take by the throat, put on the neck';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hanaqa 'strangle', Heb. hanaq, Aram. hanaq, Syr. hanaq, Md. hnq, Ug. hnq, Akk. hanaqu. ETHIOPIC: Tna. hanaqa 'strangle', Te. hanaqa, Arg. hannaqa, Har. hanaqa, Amh. annaqa, Gur. anaqa.

phonetics: hansasa (T.M) hansasa (Gr.54) handada 'break the pods of beans or peas with the teeth in order to eat the legume, sift, separate, draw a line (to separate), make a drinking cup from an animal's horns'; tahansasa, passive; hansas (K*) 'broken open pod';
meaning: 'break the pods of beans or peas with the teeth in order to eat the legume, sift, separate, draw a line (to separate), make a drinking cup from an animal's horns';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hasas 'crack, crevice', Heb. hasas 'divide in groups', PBh. hasas 'separate'. ETHIOPIC: Te. hansasa 'cut off the branches', Tna. hansasa 'break the shell with the teeth, mark a border (to separate it from another)', Amh. anuttata 'scrape, smooth by scraping'. See also hss, hasasa, hanata, vantata.

phonetics: hanzir (pl. hanzir) (Lt), hanzar, hanzir, hanzir (D.T.M) 'pig, wild boar'; hanzira (Lt) 'whip of pigskin';
origin_explanation: from Ar. hinzir (considered by Fraenkel 110 an Aramaic loanword; see also Jeffery 126). Tna. hanzir; in the other Semitic languages: Heb. hazir, Aram. hazira, Syr. hazira, Md. hizura, Ug. hnzr, Akk. huziru.

meaning: T.M) harif ሐሪፍ 'current year, (T.M) last year, (K*) season of the year'; harifa, harifa-ni 'this year' (adv.).
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. harif 'autumn, year', SAr. hrf 'autumn, year'. Heb. horep 'harvest time', Akk. harpu '(early) harvest, summer'. For the relation between 'winter, rainy season' and 'year', cp. Som. gù 'rainy season, year'. Amh. karrāmä 'spend the rainy season, spend the year'. See also harif.

meaning: L. harama ሐረመ (K*) 'sting, prick'; harmat 'letter, leg of a letter, single stroke made in forming a letter, word set off by double dots serving as word separators, min- ute letter of the law'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. harama 'slit, crack, perforate', the trac- ing of the letter being perceived as perforation. For 'slit', cp. also Sh. harmet 'notch in the ear or in the lid'. Noeldeke 1904:133, n. 1 connects it with the name of the letter harm (h). Ambros suggests a borrowing from Gk. grammata 'letters' (pl. of gram- ma) since Gk. g is taken over as g in Arabic and Ar. g normally becomes h in Geez in Arabic loanwords. ETHIOPIC: Amh. harmāt, same as Geez.
perhaps for harum 'forbidden, taboo' referring to the prohibition of eating the sinew of the thigh vein in Gen. 32:32 (Getatchew).

meaning: L. harasa ሐረሰ (T) harasa ሐረሰ 'bear a child, lie in bed when giving birth to a child, be with child or with young (cattle)'; harrāsa (Y) 'assist in childbed'; 'ahrāsa, (L) 'ahrāsa 'assist in childbed'; harās, (T) harās, (L) harās 'who is with child or with young';
origin_explanation: (possibly where a woman in childbed retires); or could it be a variant of 'arīs (for which see above)?'; 'ahrāsi, māhrasi (Gr) 'who assists in childbed'. SEMITIC: Ar. harās 'woman in the first period of pregnancy'. Akk. harāšu 'be a woman in confine- ment'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. harāsä(t) 'give birth to a child', Te. harās 'woman in childbed'. Har. harās, Ar. Amh.Gaf.Gur. aras; passed into Cushitic: Bil. harās 'give birth to'. See also harasa I.

meaning: L. harasa I (yəhras) ሐረሰ, (T.TS) harasa (KG) harrasa ሐረሰ 'feed, nourish, take good care of someone by properly feeding, fatten, (T.Y) pamper, spoil'; perhaps originally 'take good care of a woman in childbed' leading to the general meaning of taking care of anyone; 'ahrāsa, (T) 'ahrāsa 'nourish well, (T) bring up'; taharāsa, passive of harāsa; harāši (K*), act. part. of harāsa;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. harrasa 'feed on the occasion of childbirth'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. arrāsä 'take care of a parturient woman'. harasa is separated here from harāsa (as does Dillmann 587), but in fact it is probably the same root with a different spelling and means 'to bear a child' and 'feed (or take care of) the woman in childbed'.

meaning: L. harasa II ሐረሰ (Y) = Amh. qällämä 1) 'stain, dye'; 2) 'catch a ball'.
L. harasa ሐረሰ 'cut in, carve, engrave, incise, write'; taharasa, passive;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. harasa 'cleave, strip off, scrape off'. SAr. hrs 'valuation (of agricultural products)'. Heb. hārās 'slice', hārās 'decide' (see Akk. below). hārāṣ 'cut a trench', Aram. hārās 'cut into', Ug. hrs 'cut' (Caquot 441). Akk. harāsu 'cut, cut off, en- grave, decide, determine' (see Heb. hārās, above). See on this root Marrassigni 130ff. ETHIOPIC: Gaf.Gur. arrātä 'cut'; perhaps also Amh. harāṣäṣä alä (from harāṣ < *hrs/t with reduplica- tion of the last two radicals) 'make a grinding or grating noise'. In view of the Semitic root hrs 'cut off, incise' there is no need to consider the Gaf.Gur.

meaning: L. hars 'confinement of a woman in childbed';
T) harsi 'state of a woman in childbed';
L. haris (with h in KG) 'small hut, secluded place
origin_explanation: root a hybrid between *hrd 'slaughter' and q*r't 'cut' as suggested by Polotsky, JAOS 69 (1949).41.

meaning: L. haret ሐረት, harit ሐሪት; see harya.

L. harawa (yəhru) ሐረወ, (T.K*) harawa ሐረወ 'pierce, perforate, make a hole, carve, engrave, chisel, make a sketch, (Y.K*) dig, dig out, excavate'; 'ahrāwa, causative; also 'engrave'; taharawa, (L) taharawa, passive of harawa; harāwi, act. part. of harawa; also 'sculptor'; haro (K*), haro (T) 'chisel'; hərāw, hərāw 'perforated, carved, engraved, chiseled'; harwāt 'act of engraving, act of carving, carving work, hollow, cavity, chisel, engraving tool, clasp';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. hurr 'mouth of a millstone' (that is, 'hollow, cavity'). Heb. hor 'hole', Syr. hūrā 'hole', hērīṭā 'ditch, trench' (considered by Brockel- mann 1928:208 an Akkadian loanword). Ug. hry (from hry) 'grave'. Akk. herū, harāru 'dig, dig out'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. harāwā 'open' (could it be a metathe- sis of rhw?). Amh. māro 'chisel' (ancient form mahro, mahro; see Dillmann 588, under harwāt). Gur. m'arā. māro, from *məhraw (Praetorius 1879:157).

meaning: L. herawa ሐረወ 'be good, be benevolent, be excellent';
L. hīrut 'goodness, kindness, benevolence, excel- lence, virtue, good quality, integrity, benefit, preference, (Lt) merit, good action'; hirəwənnā, hirunā, (Lt) herwənā 'goodness, be- neficence';
origin_explanation: the verb is a denominative from her (see hayara).

meaning: L. harya (yəhray, yəhri) ሐረየ 'choose, discern, elect, select'; 'ahrāya, causative; taharya, passive; 'astaharya (Gr.206) 'choose with discrimina- tion';
fem. harit) 'chosen, elect, selected, excel- lent, pleasing, acceptable, preferable, arbiter, mediator';

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. hyr, in tahayyara, ihtāra 'choose, select'. Akk. hiyāru 'choose'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. harāyä 'choose', Te. hara (hry).

meaning: L. haray ሐረይ 'In Proverbs 8:19 (of the Asmara Bible) is probably a misprint for fəräya ፍሬየ 'my fruit'; indeed the Amharic Old Testament has fəräyä 'my fruit'. Or could it be a misprint for haruy 'chosen, preferable'? (Getatchew).

L. hasra (yahsar) ሐሰረ, (KG) hasra ሐሰረ 'be disgraced, suffer disgrace, be wretched, be mis- erable, be reduced to poverty, be dishonored, be confounded, be vilified, endure hardship, be humbled, be brought low, be abased, be despised, languish, be wasted (corn), be de- pleted (land)'; hassara 'treat with indignity, treat with igno- miny'; 'ahsara 'debase, reduce to disgrace, abuse, humble, humiliate, expose to humiliation, de- grade, dishonor, treat with ignominy, despise, depreciate, shame, bring to nothing, bring low, afflict'; 'uhsara 'he degraded himself, he dis- honored himself'; tahasra 'be afflicted with abuse, be treated with ignominy'; 'astahasara (Lt) 'disgrace'; hasur 'wretched, miserable impoverished, des- picable, despised, vile, defective, afflicted, worth- less, contemptible, of lowly position, de- graded, low, dishonorable'; hasār 'dishonor, wretchedness, abominable
'lowness, baseness'; 'decline, impoverishment, being reduced to humiliation'

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. haṣira 'suffer loss, lose, be beguiled', SAr. perhaps hṣr 'poorer class', Soq. di-hoṣir 'cheap', Šḥ. ḥsor 'lose', Heb. ḥāser 'diminish, decrease', Aram. ḥāsar 'diminish', ḥasser 'lessen, deprive', Syr. ḥassar 'decrease, be in want', Md. hṣr 'lack, be defective', Ug. ḥṣr. Akk. ḥašāru 'break off'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. haṣarä 'be humiliated, be in distress, be used up (clothes), go down in price', Te. haṣra 'become of inferior value', Amh. aṣar, haṣar 'affliction, misfortune', aḥṣäro 'humiliation' (from Geez).
'haṣarsara 'revile repeatedly' from hṣr (see above) with reduplication of the last two radicals.

phonetics: (yaḥṣaʾa) ኀጸአ ኀጽአ, (T) haṣʾa ሐጽአ, (L) haṣʾa ሐጽአ
meaning: 'be calm, be still, be appeased, cool off (anger), subside, be faint'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. haṣaʾa 'be humble, be submissive, be faint (voice)', perhaps Heb. hāšā (hṣy) 'keep silence', Aram. hāšā, ḥāšī 'be silent, be quiet'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. ʾaṣṣ bäla 'be quiet', Amh. ʾaṣṣ silence!', Te. ḥass. Gur. aṣ; also Cushitic: Or.Som. us, Sa. ʾaṣä 'the being quiet'.

meaning: 'be scabby, be scurfy, be covered with a rash, (K) itch, peel, scratch, (T.M) widen (wound)'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. haṣifa 'be infected by scabies'.

meaning: 'seek, seek out, look for, search, explore, desire, wish, inquire, demand, entreat, scrutinize, investigate, pursue'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. haṣṣa (Gr.205) 'seek after, investigate'; Ethiopic: Tna. haṣaṣa, passive, reflexive; also 'dispute, debate, demand'.

meaning: 'inquire collectively, discuss with one another, seek one another, have a controversy with one another, provoke one another'
'inquiry, searching, request, claim, petition, expectation, wish, desire, investigation'
'who inquires diligently, who scrutinizes, who investigates'

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Akk. haṣāhu (from hṣ-hṣ) 'desire, require', Syr. haṣāh 'be needed'. ETHIOPIC: Te. haṣṣa (hṣṣ) 'watch, pay attention', mäḥaṣaṣ 'spy', Tna. haṣāṣä 'seek, search', ʾaṣṣāṣä 'scout, examine, look for'. For Haṣaṣa 'desire, wish, want', Gur. käṣe, see Leslau 19. See also ib. p. 557 for ṣā, ša. The root passed into Cushitic: Bil.Sa. haṣaṣ 'spy, investigate'.

meaning: 'gravel', Te. hoṣa, hoṣāʾt, Amh. haṣa 'resembling coarse white sand', hoṣa 'sand' (from Geez)
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. hoṣa 'gravel', Te. hoṣa, hoṣāʾt, Amh. haṣa 'resembling coarse white sand', hoṣa 'sand' (from Geez). The identification of Ar. aṣwāʾ 'sand' with G. hoṣa suggested by Praetorius 1879:82 is unlikely. Amh. Aṣäwa could be identified with G. hoṣa only if it were borrowed from a Cushitic language where ṣ > s > š. The root passed into Cushitic: Sa. hoṣe, höṣe.
SEMITIC: related to Ar. haṣada 'twist'. ETHIOPIC: Reinisch 1895:34 compares Be. ʾaṣda 'ring' (< ʾaṣda, kaṣda).

meaning: 'iron, sword, weapon, breastplate, iron tool (axe, razor, nail)'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. haṣīn 'axe' (considered by Fraenkel an Aramaic loanword), Soq. heṣihin, Aram. haṣṣīnā, Syr. haṣṣīnā, Akk. haṣṣinnu. According to Zimmern 12 (contrary to Kaufman 54) Akk. haṣṣinnu probably Aram. > Ar.Geez. On its connection with Heb. ḥarzen 'axe', Ar. karzan < *k/garsin, Salonen, SO 17, 2 (1952), 8, and Baumgartner For more details on haṣṣin, see Furlani, RSE 6 (1947),1-11. On Md. qurnaṣa 'axe' < Akk. haṣṣinnu see Dietrich 303. ETHIOPIC: Tna. haṣīn 'iron', ḥənṣi (with metathesis), Te. haṣīn, Amh. haṣṣin (from Geez); passed into Cushitic: Aw.Kham. aṣīn 'iron, weapon'.

SEMITIC: Ar. hṣr, in iḥtaṣara 'shorten', hinṣir 'little finger', Syr. heṣrā, Md. hiṣra. For a possible connection with Heb. qṣr 'short' (with q-ṣ), see ḥadafa. Qafara II. ETHIOPIC: Tna. haṣarä 'be short', Te. haṣra, Har. haṭära, Arg. haṭṭära, Amh. aṭṭära, Gur. aṭärä.

meaning: (H 219 wrongly translates G. haṣawa 'decide' on the basis of Amh. qʷärräṭä, translation of haṣawa. It is true that Amh. qʷärräṭä also means 'decide', but 'decide' is a figurative meaning of 'cut')
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. haṣā (hṣy) 'castrate', haṣīy 'eunuch', Heb. hāṣā (hṣy) 'divide', PbH. hāṣā (hṣy) 'split', Syr. haṣṣā (hṣy) 'pluck out, tear out', Akk. eṣū 'slit'. Noeldeke 1910:103 considers Ar. haṣā a denominative from huṣya 'testicle'. This may be the case for Ar. haṣā, but not for G. haṣawa since Geez does not have the noun hṣy for 'testicle'. See on this root Rundgren 1968:163-4. ETHIOPIC: Tna. haṣaw 'eunuch' (from Geez), Amh. haṣaw (from Geez); passed into Cushitic: Bil. aṣaw 'castrate'.

meaning: 'seal, seal up, stamp, impress, engrave, mark, sign, coin (money), tattoo, prick, anoint with holy oil, sanction with a seal, shut, close (and seal), end'

'seal' (v), denominative from hātamā 'seal' (n.), of Aramaic origin according to Fraenkel 252; SAr. (Qat) htm 'seal', Heb. hōtām (for its Egyptian origin, see Ellenbogen 74, with bibliography), Aram. hātam 'seal' (v.), Syr. ḥotām, Md. htm, Ph. htm.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hatama 'seal' (v), denominative from hātamā 'seal' (n.), of Aramaic origin according to Fraenkel 252; SAr. (Qat) htm 'seal', Heb. hōtām (for its Egyptian origin, see Ellenbogen 74, with bibliography), Aram. hātam 'seal' (v.), Syr. ḥotām, Md. htm, Ph. htm. ETHIOPIC: Tna. hatāmā 'seal' (v.), Te. hatma 'seal, stamp, print', Arg. hattāma, Amh. atṭāmā, Gur. atāmā.

meaning: (T) hatata 'be kindled, burn, be alight, shine'; aḥṭawa 'light a torch or a lamp, burn, kindle, emit light, give light'; hatāw, (KG) hatṭāw 'lit, burning, shining'; māḥ(ə)ṭot (pl. maḥāṭəw, maḥāṭawāt) 'lamp, light, torch, candlestick'; (maḥāṭawa hosənnā 'eve of Palm Sunday'); (maḥāṭawa bəʿāla lədat 'Christmas eve'); (sanbata maḥāṭawa fāsəkā 'Easter eve'); (wāzemā maḥāṭəw 'hymns sung on the eve of a holiday'); maḥṭāw, maḥṭāw, same as māḥ(ə)ṭot.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Heb. ḥātā (ḥtw/y) 'kindle, ignite' (Barth 1902:24), maḥtā 'fire pan, censer', that is, 'a utensil that serves as receptacle for burning things' (Eitan 1924:25), Pbh. hiṭṭā (ḥty) 'stir embers, rake', Aram. ḥāṭā, ḥāṭī 'stir embers, take coal out with a pan'. ETHIOPIC: Har. maḥtīt (maxṭūt) 'light-giving object', maḥtīt (from Cerulli 1936:262) gives the impression of coming from maḥāṭīt with ḥ > k in loanwords, Amh. maḥṭot, matot (from Geez); passed into Cushitic: Or. makṭuṭa, Af. makṭuṭ.

meaning: act. part. of haṭa; also 'sinner, sinful, wicked'; haṭāw 'wanting, deprived of, indigent, needy, who is in need, who does not have, devoid of, destitute, fugitive, (Lt) that is missing'; haṭi' 'loss, lack, want, (Lt) scarcity, penury'; haṭā', haṭā' 'lack, want, scarcity'; haṭāw 'lack, want, scarcity'; haṭi'at 'lack, want, scarcity'; haṭi'at (pl. haṭāwə', haṭāyə') 'lack, fault, sin, trespass'; (za-ḥaṭi'at 'sin-offering'); haṭ(ə)āwī 'sinner'; haṭ(ə)annā 'state of wanting, the being deprived of, the being in need'; māḥ(ə)ṭā (M), māḥəṭā (D) 'fugitive(s), refugee(s)'; māḥ(ə)ṭa (Lt) 'refuge'; taḥaṭ(ə)ot 'state of non-existence, lack'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ḥaṭi'a 'sin, commit an error' (for its being borrowed from Syriac, see Schwally, ZDMG 52[1898].132; however, in view of SAr. ḥṭ' 'commit an offense', the borrowing seems unlikely, according to W. Müller); Soq. ḥaṭa' 'sin', Sh. ḥaṭe' 'get lost', Heb. ḥāṭā (ḥṭ') 'sin, miss a mark, fail' (for Heb. ḥaṭṭā(')t 'penury' [a meaning found in Geez] in Job 14:16, see W. Thomas, JTS, n.s. 15[1964].295ff), Aram. ḥāṭā (ḥṭī), ḥāṭī 'sin', Syr. ḥaṭā (ḥṭī), Md. ḥṭa 'sin, fail', Ug. ḥṭ', Akk. ḥaṭṭu 'sin'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. ḥaṭi'a 'be wanting', cp. Tna. ḥaṭi'at, Amh. aṭiyat, Gur. haṭiat. For the meaning 'sin', the root is borrowed from Aramaic (Noeldeke 1910:36).

meaning: 'lack, be deprived of, not find, miss, be in want of, not have, be bereaved, be destitute, be desolate, fail, (Y) sin'; aḥṭa'a 'bereave, cause to lack, deprive, cause to sin'; taḥaṭa 'lack, be wanting, be missing, be deprived of, withdraw, escape, flee, vanish, be absent, be lost, disappear, withdraw, shun'; (ṭaḥaṭa 'əmənna 'lose').

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. ḥaṭīṭ 'thin, small' (Dillmann Praetorius 1890:24 derives it rightly from the Semitic root ḥṭ (ḥnṭ) 'corn, rye' which took the meaning of 'grain' (note, however, that Geez has ḥṭot ḥṭ). Ar. ḥinṭa 'wheat', Soq. ḥinṭeh, Heb. ḥiṭṭā, Aram. ḥinṭāṭā, ḥiṭṭāṭā, Syr. ḥaṭṭāṭā, Md. ḥṭa, Ug. ḥṭ-t, Akk. uṭṭatu; probably also Cushitic: ḥadūd 'corn'. See also ḥədāt, ḥiṭ.

ETHIOPIC: Tna. haṇṭaṭa 'pick off one by one', that is, 'make small'. For 'small', cp. Te.Tna. hənṭaṭ 'little child', from hṭ(t) with augmented n. The root also occurs in Cushitic: Bil. hinṭan 'be thin, weak, delicate', Kham. iṭin, eṭin.

meaning: 'seduce, persuade'; secondary form of hyṭ, heṭa.
(K*) 'open'; hawawa (K*) 'window'; seems to be reconstructed from hawāḥəw.

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ḥawār 'young unweaned camel', ḥūr (from ḥwr) 'deficiency', Mh. ḥawr 'few, little', Heb. ḥwr in ḥāru (Is.24:6) 'become little, waste away'. ETHIOPIC: Te. hawāra 'lose the voice owing to weakness', Te. ḥəwār 'young (of camel and donkey)'. Bil. ḥuwer is borrowed from Arabic ḥuwār.

meaning: 'mix, mingle, confuse'; taḥawəša 'make fellowship (with), have intercourse'; hawəwəs, hawwəs 'mixed, confused, confederate'; hawš 'mixture, confusion, blending, fusion'; hušat 'act of mixing, mingling fusion blend, ing, mixture'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. hawāšā 'mingle', hawəswas 'mixed', Amh. hawāšā 'mix'.
SEMITIC: related to Ar. (Dat) ḥād (ḥwd) 'agitate, shake'.

meaning: 'become well, become strong, have the upper hand, be mighty, powerful, prevail, (T.TS.M) utter cries of joy, boast, (K*) 'shout for help'; hayyala 'become strong, exert strength, overcome, overpower, prevail over, be superior to, give power'; tahayyala, causative; also 'strengthen'; taḥayyala 'become strong, be strengthened, be powerful, have power, get the upper hand, force (one's way), gain mastery, do by force, take by force, use violence, be violent, overcome by violence, tyrannize, oppress, dominate, be empowered, make an effort, strive'; taḥayyala 'encourage one another'; astahayyala, causative of tahayyala; hayyāl 'strong, powerful, mighty, prevailing, champion, active, valiant, violent'; hayyāl, same; hayl 'power, strength, might, force, valor, virtue, severity, firmness'; (ba-hayl 'violently, mightily'); haylāt (pl. of hayl) 'duties, faculties, exploits, mighty works, army, troops, hosts'; mastahayyəl, (Gr) mastahayyālī 'violent, oppressor'; taḥayyəlī 'oppressor'; taḥayyəlo(t) 'duress, sternness, harsh treatment, force'; (bataḥayyəlo 'impetuously'); taḥayyəlt 'duress, violence, oppression'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ḥayl, ḥawl 'power, strength' (note ḥ as against h of Geez), SAr. hyl, Heb. ḥayil, Aram. ḥelā, Syr. ḥaylā, Md. ḥaila, Ug. ḥl 'army', perhaps Akk. illatu 'army'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. hayālā 'be strong', Te. hela, Amh. ayyālā. For 'power', cp. Tna. hayli, Te. hil, Amh.Gur. hayl; passed into Cushitic: Bil. hayil 'strong', Sa. haylā 'power'.

SEMITIC: Ar. ḥayma 'tent' (considered by Fraenkel 30 of uncertain origin, but possibly from Geez. Vollers 1896:631 thinks that it is of Persian origin, but not so Sandberg 1920:674ff.; see also Jeffery 127), SAr. (Minaean) hym (pl) 'cabins' given by Conti Rossini 1931:155 is not recorded in the recent dictionaries of South Arabic; Ug. ḥmt 'tent' (Gordon 403), 'enclosure, sheepfold' (Caquot 513). ETHIOPIC: Te. haymāt 'round hut made of branches' (Te. kāymāt is an Arabic loanword), Amh. haymāt; passed into Cushitic: Bil. kaymat (from Te. kāymāt).
from Gk. hēbaō 'attain puberty, be in the prime of youth' (Müller).

meaning: (Y), hera (K) 'be good' her (fem. hert; pl.m. herān, pl.f. herāt) 'good, excellent, good thing'; herawa, see above; hirat (K) 'goodness';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hayr 'good'; SAr. hyr 'nobleman', Akk. hiāru, hāru 'choose'.ETHIOPIC: Tna. heray 'good'; also ker, sär. Te. käyyära 'select', ker 'good luck' (from Ar. hayr), Amh. ǝr 'generous, good' (see Praetorius 1879:67), Gur. ker. See also herawa.hisān 'goodness, excellence, superiority, preponderance'; hay(ya)sännä (Lt) 'excellence, goodly appearance';ETHIOPIC: Tna. hayäsä, hesä 'be better'. Te. hayyäsa; passed into Cushitic: Be. hayis 'better', Af. ays.

meaning: 'be suitable'; the object suffix pronouns when attached to it have the meaning of an indirect object; e.g. hesanni 'it is suitable for me' (or 'it suits me'); hayyäsa 'be better, be suitable'; with object suffix pronouns: yəheyyašä 'he is better than she', təheyyašäkki 'she is better for you'; 'ahyasa, 'ahesa 'do good things, make something good, make something better'; 'ahayyäsa 'make better, make more pleasing, regard as better, comfort'; tahayyäsa 'be placed first, be placed as better'; 'astahayyäsa 'prefer, regard as preferable, regard as better, praise, ameliorate'; hayyus (Lt) 'better';

(Gr.458) hayata (T) heta 'deceive, cheat, seduce, corrupt, entice, persuade, convince, flatter, urge (with words)'; tahayta, taheta, passive; hayāti, (T) hayāti, act. part. of heta; hayyut, pass. part. of heta; also 'treacherous'; hitat 'trick, deception, seduction'; hitān 'seduction, enticing, persuading, artifice, ruse';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. hitan 'flattering' (from Geez). The derivation of heta from a noun *ht 'mistake', suggested by Praetorius 1890:27, is unlikely. See also hetawa.

meaning: (Y.H.M) 'be quiet, be tranquil'; for haza , variant of had'a (see above).
(T) 'sink into something soft'; probably instead of hazaza and to be compared with hazhaza.

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ḥasira 'suffer loss, lose, be beguiled'. SAr. perhaps ḥsr 'poorer class'. Soq. di-ḥosir 'cheap'. Šḥ. ḥsor 'lose'. Heb. ḥāser 'diminish, decrease', Aram. ḥāsar 'diminish', ḥasser 'lessen, deprive', Syr. ḥasar 'decrease, be in want', Md. ḥsr 'lack, be defective', Ug. ḥsr. Akk. ḥašāru 'break off'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. ḥasāra 'be humiliated, be in distress, be used up (clothes), go down in price', Te. ḥasra 'become of inferior value', Amh. aṣar, ḥasar 'affliction, misfortune', aḥsəro 'humiliation' (from Geez).
aḥsarsara 'revile repeatedly' from ḥsr (see above) with reduplication of the last two radicals.

meaning: 'be calm, be still, be appeased, cool off (anger), subside, be faint'; aḥšā'a, causative;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ḥaša'a 'be humble, be submissive, be faint (voice)', perhaps Heb. ḥāšā (ḥšy) 'keep silence'. Aram. ḥāšā, ḥāšī 'be silent, be quiet'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. aṣṣ bālā 'be quiet', Amh. aṣṣ 'silence!', Te. ḥass. Gur. aṣ; also Cushitic: Or.Som. uṣ, aṣṣ 'the being quiet'.

meaning: (T.M) ḥasafa 'be scabby, be scurfy, be covered with a rash, (K) itch, peel, scratch, (T.M) widen (wound)'; ḥasuf, (K.Gr.49) ḥasuf 'scabby, scurfy, that itches, (K*) covered with wounds'; ḥasaf (K*) 'itch, a boil';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ḥasifa 'be infected by scabies'.

meaning: 'seek, see, look out, look for, search, explore, desire, wish, inquire, demand, entreat, scrutinize, investigate, pursue'; (Gr.205) 'seek after, investigate'; tahāšāśa, tahāšāśa, passive, reflexive; also 'dispute, debate, demand';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Akk. ḥašāhu (from ḥš-ḥš) 'desire, need, require', Syr. ḥšāh 'be needed'. ETHIOPIC: Te. ḥassa (ḥss) 'watch, pay attention', maḥāšāš 'spy', Tna. ḥasāšā 'seek, search', Amh. aṣāšā 'scout, examine, look for'. For Har. xaṣa 'desire, wish, want', Gur. kāše, see Leslau 1979:355. See also ib. p. 557 for ṣā, šā. The root passed into Cushitic: Bil.Sa. ḥasas 'spy, investigate'.

meaning: 'inquire collectively, discuss with one another, seek one another, have a controversy with one another, provoke one another';

origin_explanation: aṣṭaḥasāśa, caus. of tahāšāśa;

meaning: 'inquiry, searching, request, claim, petition, expectation, wish, desire, investigation'; ḥusāše, (KG) ḥusāše 'inquiry. (KG) prediction, presentment';
'who inquires diligently, who scrutinizes, who investigates';

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. ḥaṣāšā 'sweep off', Te. ḥaṣ(s)ä- ṣā, Amh. aṣaṣā.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. aṣaṣot. See also ḥashaṣt.
SEMITIC: Ar. ḥaṣā (with ḥ) 'stone, pebble', ḥiṣiṣ 'stones', Soq. ḥaṣaḥiḥin 'gravel', Heb. ḥāṣāṣ 'pebbles, gravel', Aram. ḥāṣāṣ 'gravel', Syr. ḥaṣāṣā, aṣaṣa, aṣṣa. Akk. ḥissu.

meaning: Tna. ḥoṣā 'gravel', Te. ḥoṣa, ḥoṣaḥ, Amh. ḥaṣa 'resembling coarse white sand' (from Geez).
origin_explanation: The identification of Ar. aṣwā 'sand' with G. ḥoṣā suggested by Praetorius 1879:82 is unlikely. Amh. aṣāwā could be identified with G. ḥoṣā only if it were borrowed from Cushitic language where ṣ > s. The root passed into Cushitic: Sa. ḥoṣe, ḥoṣe.

meaning: (T.M) ḥaṣaṣ 'broom, meat hook, hooked piece of iron, tongs, fork';
'iron, sword, weapon, breastplate, iron tool (axe, razor, nail)'; (labsa ḥaṣṣin 'cuirass'); ḥaṣṣāwənt (pl.) 'iron tools';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ḥaṣin 'axe' (considered by Fraenkel an Aramaic loanword), Soq. ḥeṣiḥin, Aram. ḥaṣṣīnā, Syr. ḥaṣṣīnā, Akk. ḥaṣṣinnu. According to Zimmern 12 (contrary to Kaufman 54) Akk. ḥaṣṣinnu probably Aram. > Ar.Geez. On its connection with Heb. garzen 'axe', Ar. karzan < k/garsin, Salonon, SO 17, 2 (1952), 8, and Baumgartner For more details on ḥaṣṣin, see Furlani, RSE 6 (1947),1-11. On Md. qurnaṣa 'axe' < Akk. ḥaṣṣinnu see Dietrich 303. ETHIOPIC: Tna. ḥaṣin 'iron', ḥənṣi (with metathesis), Te. ḥaṣin, Amh. ḥaṣṣin (from Geez); passed into Cushitic: Aw.Kham. ačin 'iron, weapon'.

meaning: 'twist, make tight, bend, curve'; ḥəṣāṣ, ḥəṣāṣ (pl. ḥəṣāṣādət, ḥəṣāṣādət), ḥoṣāṣ (pl. ḥoṣāṣādət) 'ring, hook, clasp, twisted';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. ḥaṣada 'twist'. ETHIOPIC: Reinisch 1895:34 compares Be. aṣa 'ring' (< aṣda, kaṣda).

meaning: 'be short, be shortened, be brief'; aḥṣara 'shorten, abridge, abbreviate, diminish, (Lt) hold in low esteem, depreciate'; aṣṭaḥaṣara 'shorten, diminish, reduce, abbreviate'; ḥaṣar 'shortness, shortage'; ḥəṣur 'short, shortened, brief, restricted'; (ḥəṣura, ba-ḥəṣur 'in a few words, briefly'); ḥaṣir (fem. ḥaṣṣār), (T) ḥaṣṣir 'short, brief'; ḥuṣāre (Gr) 'shortness'; ḥaṣrat 'brevity, shortness'; aḥṣəro 'abridgment, abbreviation'; (ba-aḥṣəro 'briefly');
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ḥṣr, in iḥtaṣara 'shorten', ḥinṣir 'little finger', Syr. ḥeṣrā, Md. ḥiṣra. For a possible connection with Heb. qṣr 'short' (with q-ṣ), see ḥadafa, qafara II. ETHIOPIC: Tna. ḥaṣāra 'be short', Te. ḥaṣra, Har. ḥaṭṭāra, Arg. ḥaṭṭāra, Amh. aṭṭārā, Gur. aṭṭārā.

meaning: (H 219 translates G. ḥaṣawa 'decide' on the basis of Amh. qərrāṭā, translation of ḥasawa. It is true that Amh. qərrāṭā also means 'decide', but 'decide' is a figurative meaning of 'cut'); aḥṣawa (K*), causative; taḥaṣawa 'be made a eunuch, be castrated'; ḥaṣaw 'eunuch, chamberlain'; ḥaṣwat 'act of emasculating';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ḥaṣā (ḥṣy) 'castrate', ḥaṣīy 'eunuch', Heb. ḥāṣā (ḥṣy) 'divide', PbH. ḥāṣā (ḥṣy) 'split', Syr. ḥaṣyā (ḥṣy) 'pluck out, tear out', Akk. eṣū 'slit'. Noeldeke 1910:103 considers Ar. ḥaṣā a denominative from ḥuṣya 'testicle'. This may be the case for Ar. ḥaṣā, but not for G. ḥaṣawa since Geez does not have the noun ḥṣy for 'testicle'. See on this root Rundgren 1968:163-4. ETHIOPIC: Tna. ḥaṣəw 'eunuch' (from Geez), Amh. ḥaṣəw (from Geez); passed into Cushitic: Bil. aṣəw 'castrate'.

meaning: 'seal, seal up, stamp, impress, engrave, mark, sign, coin (money), tattoo, prick, anoint with holy oil, sanction with a seal, shut, close (and seal), end'; aḥtama, causative; ḥatāmi, act. part. of ḥatama; ḥatum 'sealed, stamped, coined, signed, marked, shut'; ḥatām (Gr.458) 'sealing' (n); ḥatāmat 'sealing' (n); maḥ(ə)tam, maḥ(ə)təm (pl. maḥ(ə)tamāt, māḥ(ə)tamāt, maḥātəm) 'stamp, seal, mark, impression, sign, (Lt) letter (by extension), closure, prison'; (maḥ(ə)tama rāšān 'hell', lit. 'closure [prison] of the wicked'); maḥatamāt (T.M) 'golden chain' (read maḥā- təm);

origin_explanation: from Gk. hēbaō 'attain puberty, be in the prime of youth' (Müller).

meaning: (Y), hera (K) 'be good' her (fem. hert; pl.m. herān, pl.f. herāt) 'good, excellent, good thing'; herawa, see above; hirat (K) 'goodness';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hayr 'good'; SAr. hyr 'nobleman', Akk. hiāru, hāru 'choose'.ETHIOPIC: Tna. heray 'good'; also ker, sär. Te. käyyära 'select', ker 'good luck' (from Ar. hayr), Amh. ǝr 'generous, good' (see Praetorius 1879:67), Gur. ker. See also herawa.hisān 'goodness, excellence, superiority, preponderance'; hay(ya)sännä (Lt) 'excellence, goodly appearance';ETHIOPIC: Tna. hayäsä, hesä 'be better'. Te. hayyäsa; passed into Cushitic: Be. hayis 'better', Af. ays.

meaning: 'be suitable'; the object suffix pronouns when attached to it have the meaning of an indirect object; e.g. hesanni 'it is suitable for me' (or 'it suits me'); hayyäsa 'be better, be suitable'; with object suffix pronouns: yəheyyašä 'he is better than she', təheyyašäkki 'she is better for you'; 'ahyasa, 'ahesa 'do good things, make something good, make something better'; 'ahayyäsa 'make better, make more pleasing, regard as better, comfort'; tahayyäsa 'be placed first, be placed as better'; 'astahayyäsa 'prefer, regard as preferable, regard as better, praise, ameliorate'; hayyus (Lt) 'better';

(Gr.458) hayata (T) heta 'deceive, cheat, seduce, corrupt, entice, persuade, convince, flatter, urge (with words)'; tahayta, taheta, passive; hayāti, (T) hayāti, act. part. of heta; hayyut, pass. part. of heta; also 'treacherous'; hitat 'trick, deception, seduction'; hitān 'seduction, enticing, persuading, artifice, ruse';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. hitan 'flattering' (from Geez). The derivation of heta from a noun *ht 'mistake', suggested by Praetorius 1890:27, is unlikely. See also hetawa.

meaning: (Y.H.M) 'be quiet, be tranquil'; for haza , variant of had'a (see above).
(T) 'sink into something soft'; probably instead of hazaza and to be compared with hazhaza.
